title: practical english usage - Part 299
content:
What do you have to do to become a pilot?

It was becoming very dark.

can       be     used         before           adjectives                 and       noun         phrases.

etcbecome        dark,     become         a  pilot, depend             partly          on     grammar,              partly          on     meaning               and       partly          on     fixed         usage.

talk       about          changes.             The    differences                  between              them         are      complicated                    - they and             can       all     be     used        with        similar            meanings                to Become, get, go, come, grow  turn 394 become,        get,    go,   grow,      etc:  changes Word  Problems from A  to Z Section 31 vocabulary •  394   become, get, go, grow, etc: changes In the Greek legend, Circe turned men into pigs.

Turn (in)to can also be used to talk about changing one thing into another.

At the end of her life she turned Catholic.

Towards the end of the war he turned traitor.

He worked in a bank for thirty years before turning painter.

turn with a noun (with no preposition or article) or an adjective.

To  talk about a change of occupation, religion, politics, etc, we  sometimes use They stood there as if they had been turned (in)to stone.

Everything that King Midas touched turned (in)to gold.

Turn to and turn into can both be used before the names of materials.

A girl has to kiss a lot of frogs before one of them turns into a prince.

He's a lovely man, but when he gets jealous he turns into a monster.

Turn into is used before nouns.

I turned fifty last week. It's all downhill from now on.

We can use turn before numbers to talk about important changes of age.

He turns violent after he's had a couple of drinks.

She turned bright red and ran out of the room.

colour words (and is not so informal as go).

Turn is used mostly for visible or striking changes of state. It is common before 7  turn     red,    etc He grew to accept his stepmother, but he never grew to love her.

attitude, especially if these are gradual.

Grow + infinitive can be used (like come + infinitive) to talk about changes in As the weather grows colder, I think of moving to a warmer country.

When they grew rich they began to drop their old friends.

Without noticing it he grew old.

It is more formal than get or go, and a little old-fashioned or literary.

Grow is used before adjectives especially to talk about slow and gradual changes.

6 grow      old,    etc You will come to regret your decision.

I slowly came to realise that she knew what she was doing.

Come + infinitive can be used to talk about changes in mental state or attitude.

Trust me - it will all come right in the end.

I'll make all your dreams come true.

The  most common are come right (BrE) and come true.

Come is used in a few fixed expressions to talk about things finishing up all right.

5  come      true,     etc Note that we  use get, not go, with old, tired and ill.

He went bald in his twenties. The car keeps going wrong.

go flat.

sour, bread goes stale; beer, lemonade, musical instruments and car tyres horses go lame; machines go wrong; meat, fish or vegetables go bad; milk goes changes for the worse. People go mad (BrE), crazy, deaf, blind, grey or bald; Go (not usually get) is used before adjectives in some expressions that refer to b  changes for the worse vocabulary •  396   before: adverb She realised that she had seen him  before.                 -> are talking about'. In this case a past perfect tense is used.

Before can also mean ‘ at any time before then - before the past moment that we I think I've seen this film before. Have you ever been here before?

perfect tense is normally used.

We   can use before to mean ‘ at any time before now'. In British English, a present 1 'at  any   time    before     now/then' 396 before:      adverb For be gone, ►    477.

'Where's Lucy? 'She's gone to London.' - I've been to London three times this week.

Megan's come, so we can start work. (She has come and is still here.) - The electrician's already been. (He has come  and gone away again.) Note that been is only used for completed visits. Compare: Have you ever been to Northern Ireland?
